Brand.tagline: Portfolio optimization based on Markowitz theory

target-return.label: Target Return
target-return.description: Minimum risk for a specific return

target-risk.label: Target Risk
target-risk.description: Maximum return for a specific risk level

knee-point.label: Inflection Point
knee-point.description: Point of maximum curvature on the frontier

HomePage.heroDescription: Find the optimal asset allocation using Markowitz mean-variance theory. Visualize the efficient frontier and maximize your risk-adjusted return.
HomePage.feature1Description: Visualize the optimal set of portfolios that offer the highest return for each level of risk.
HomePage.feature2Description: From Max Sharpe to Inflection Point. Choose the strategy that best fits your profile.
HomePage.feature3Description: Probability of negative return, historical volatility, and confidence metrics for informed decisions.

EfficientFrontierList.title: Efficient Frontier
EfficientFrontierList.emptyTitle: You haven't run any optimizations yet
EfficientFrontierList.emptyHint: Press "Optimization" to configure your first portfolio
EfficientFrontierList.signInTitle: Sign in to view your optimizations
EfficientFrontierList.signInDescription: Create an account or sign in to save and view your portfolio optimizations.

SimulationDetail.signInTitle: Sign in to view this optimization
SimulationDetail.signInDescription: Optimizations are private. Sign in to see yours.

NewOptimization.signInTitle: Sign in to create an optimization
NewOptimization.signInDescription: You need an account to run and save your portfolio optimizations.
NewOptimization.errorOptimize: Error while optimizing. Check the assets and try again.
NewOptimization.title: Portfolio Configuration
NewOptimization.dateRangeInfoIntro: Defines the historical data period used to compute expected returns, volatilities, and correlations between assets.
NewOptimization.dateRangeInfoStart: First month of the analysis period. Data is taken from the first day of the selected month.
NewOptimization.dateRangeInfoEnd: Last month of the analysis period. Data is taken through the last day of the selected month.
NewOptimization.dateRangeInfoTip: A 3 to 5 year window strikes a good balance between capturing market trends and avoiding stale data. Longer windows smooth volatility but may include market conditions that are no longer relevant.
NewOptimization.strategyInfoIntro: Choose how you want to optimize your portfolio according to Markowitz theory:
NewOptimization.strategyMaxSharpeText: Maximizes risk-adjusted return using the Sharpe ratio. Ideal for getting the best balance between risk and return.
NewOptimization.strategyMinRiskText: Minimizes portfolio volatility. Recommended for conservative investors.
NewOptimization.strategyMaxReturnText: Maximizes expected return without regard to risk. For aggressive investors.
NewOptimization.strategyTargetReturnText: Finds the minimum-risk portfolio that achieves a specific return.
NewOptimization.strategyTargetRiskText: Finds the maximum-return portfolio for a specific risk level.
NewOptimization.strategyInflectionText: Identifies the point of maximum curvature on the efficient frontier, where the marginal benefit of taking on more risk starts to diminish.
NewOptimization.targetRiskInfoIntro: This parameter sets the maximum volatility allowed for the portfolio. Volatility measures the dispersion of returns and is expressed as an annualized percentage.
NewOptimization.targetRiskInfoHow: The optimizer searches for the portfolio with the highest expected return whose volatility does not exceed the target. If multiple portfolios meet the constraint, the one with the highest return is selected.
NewOptimization.targetRiskInfoFeas: If the target risk is below the minimum achievable volatility (minimum-variance portfolio), the optimizer will return the lowest-risk portfolio possible. Typical values are between 10% and 25% annually.
NewOptimization.assetConstraintsInfoIntro: Lets you impose limits on the maximum weight that each individual asset can hold in the optimized portfolio.
NewOptimization.assetConstraintsInfoNone: The optimizer can assign any weight between 0% and 100% to each asset, which can result in highly concentrated portfolios.
NewOptimization.assetConstraintsInfoWith: A maximum weight per asset is set, forcing greater diversification. For example, a 30% cap ensures no single asset represents more than that share of the portfolio.
NewOptimization.wMaxInfoIntro: This parameter limits the maximum concentration that a single asset can have in the optimized portfolio.
NewOptimization.wMaxInfoWhy: Excessive concentration in a single asset increases idiosyncratic risk. If one company or sector runs into trouble, a heavily concentrated portfolio will suffer significant losses.
NewOptimization.wMaxInfoEffect: A lower cap forces greater diversification, which generally reduces risk but may lower expected return. A higher cap lets the optimizer concentrate more capital in the assets with the best risk-return profile.

MarkowitzResults.cumulativeReturnsSubtitle: Relative evolution of each asset and the portfolio
MarkowitzResults.compareDisclaimer: * Your portfolio's volatility is approximated by a weighted average.

ProbNegReturnChart.emptyMessage: The probability of a negative return is essentially zero

Auth.promptDescription: You need an account to create and save optimizations.

Constraints.fullInvestmentBody1Pre: The full investment constraint forces the sum of all portfolio weights to be exactly
Constraints.fullInvestmentBody1Post: . In other words, all available capital must be invested in the selected assets.
Constraints.fullInvestmentBody2Pre: The optimizer can recommend keeping part of the capital in
Constraints.fullInvestmentBody2Post: (uninvested). This is useful when the model considers that holding liquidity reduces the overall portfolio risk.
Constraints.fullInvestmentWhen1: During periods of high market volatility
Constraints.fullInvestmentWhen2: When you want a more conservative strategy
Constraints.fullInvestmentWhen3: If you prefer the model to decide the optimal exposure level
Constraints.shortSellingBody1Pre: Short selling lets you "sell" assets you don't own, betting that their price will drop. In optimization, this translates to
Constraints.shortSellingBody2: Allowing short positions gives the optimizer more flexibility and can find portfolios with higher expected return or lower risk than long-only portfolios.
Constraints.shortSellingPractical1: Requires a margin account with your broker
Constraints.leverageHelp: Lets you invest more than 100% of capital (using margin)

Welcome.title: First time investing?
Welcome.description: Walk through the Top-Down guide in five stops. From the global map to your portfolio weights, in about five minutes.

Drawer.fullExperienceLink: View this stop in the full experience

Page.footer: End of the journey · Figures shown are illustrative

ZoomIntro.description: Investing like a professional doesn't start with hunting for tickers. It starts by looking at the world. We'll zoom in: from the planet to your portfolio, in five stops.

Station1.expansionDescription: Low rates, healthy growth, strong employment. Optimism dominates the mood.
Station1.contractionDescription: Activity falling, layoffs, more expensive credit. Capital seeks shelter in defensive assets.
Station1.stagflationDescription: High inflation while the economy stalls. The worst combination: savings losing real value.

Station2.drawdownCaption: Illustrative curve. Represents a hypothetical path with a recession halfway through.
Station2.conservativeDescription: Capital preservation first. Low volatility, modest returns, few large drawdowns.
Station2.moderateDescription: The classic balance. Participates in growth but cushions the blows with fixed income.
Station2.aggressiveDescription: Maximum long-term growth. Expect large drawdowns during recessions; you have to hold on.

Station3.earlyDescription: Low rates, confidence returning. Capital rotates toward cyclicals.
Station3.midDescription: Sustained growth. Leadership broadens to industrials.
Station3.lateDescription: Inflation rises, rates rise. Commodities and defensives shine.
Station3.recessionDescription: The market takes refuge in the most stable names.

Station4.alphaName: Company with a strong competitive advantage
Station4.betaName: Sector leader trading at a hot price
Station4.deltaName: Good price but a weak business

Station5.correlationDescription: Red = move together (poor diversification). Blue = move opposite (good diversification).
Station5.correlationHoverHint: Hover over a cell to interpret it.
Station5.correlationNegative: negative correlation: they complement each other well.
Station5.correlationLow: low correlation: good candidates to combine.
Station5.frontierDescription: Each point is a portfolio. The curve marks the optimums: maximum return for each level of risk.
Station5.frontierCaption: Illustration. In the real app you use your own tickers and dates.
Station5.ctaDescription: Build your ticker list, set the date range, and let the optimizer find the optimal weights on the efficient frontier.

macro.label: The world
macro.title: What economic climate are we in?
macro.tagline: Before picking a stock, pick the playing field.
macro.summary: The first zoom is the broadest. You look at the global economy and ask whether the wind is at your back.
macro.bullet1: Pick the region where you want to operate (US, Europe, Emerging Markets).
macro.bullet2: Watch the cycle: interest rates, inflation, growth.
macro.bullet3: Check market sentiment: euphoria and panic often signal turning points.

allocation.label: Your profile
allocation.title: How much risk can you take?
allocation.tagline: Before the ticker, the mix between growth and safety.
allocation.summary: Define your asset allocation: the percentage of your capital in equities (growth) vs. fixed income (stability).
allocation.bullet2: Moderate (60/40): the classic balance between growth and protection.
allocation.bullet3: Aggressive (90/10): maximum long-term return while accepting large drawdowns.

sectors.label: The sectors
sectors.title: Follow the institutional money
sectors.tagline: Not every sector wins at the same time.
sectors.summary: When rates are low, technology shines. With high inflation, energy and staples lead. Capital rotates with the cycle.
sectors.bullet1: Cyclical sectors: technology, consumer discretionary, industrials.
sectors.bullet2: Defensive sectors: consumer staples, healthcare, utilities.
sectors.bullet3: Sector rotation anticipates phase changes in the business cycle.

assets.label: The assets
assets.title: Quality and timing: the dual filter
assets.tagline: A good company at a bad price is still a bad investment.
assets.summary: Once the sector is chosen, you cross fundamental analysis (what to buy?) with technical analysis (when to enter?).
assets.bullet1: Fundamentals: solid balance sheet, competitive advantage (moat), ethical governance.
assets.bullet2: Valuation: current price vs. intrinsic value and historical average.
assets.bullet3: Technicals: support, resistance, momentum (RSI, MACD, moving averages).

portfolio.label: The portfolio
portfolio.title: Optimization and risk management
portfolio.tagline: Here's where Markowitz comes in: exact weights on the efficient frontier.
portfolio.summary: Combine lowly correlated assets to maximize the risk-adjusted return (Sharpe). And always have an exit plan.
portfolio.bullet1: Low correlations are what make diversification truly work.
portfolio.bullet2: The efficient frontier shows the best portfolio for each level of risk.
portfolio.bullet3: Define your stop-loss before entering: capital preservation is the priority.

Debug.headerSubtitle: The covariance matrix measures how the returns of assets move together. It is essential for computing portfolio risk and optimizing diversification.
Debug.step1Body: First we compute the daily log return of each asset from historical prices. Log returns are used because they are additive over time and have better statistical behavior (closer to a normal distribution).
Debug.step1FormulaDesc: Where R_t is the log return on day t and P_t is the closing price on day t
Debug.step2Body: For each asset we compute the mean (expected return) and the standard deviation (volatility) of the daily returns, and annualize them using 252 trading days per year.
Debug.step3Body: Correlation measures how synchronized the movements of two assets are. It ranges from -1 (opposite movements) to +1 (identical movements). A correlation near 0 means the assets move independently.
Debug.step3FormulaDesc: Pearson correlation between assets A and B
Debug.interpretationNegativeText: : Assets move in opposite directions (good for diversification)
Debug.step4Body: We arrange all correlations in a symmetric matrix. The diagonal is always 1 (each asset is perfectly correlated with itself).
Debug.step5Body: Finally, we build the covariance matrix by combining volatilities and correlations. The covariance between two assets is the product of their volatilities times their correlation.
Debug.step5FormulaDesc: The diagonal contains the variances (σ²) of each asset
Debug.step6Body: The covariance matrix is used to compute total portfolio risk. The formula combines each asset's weight with the covariance between every pair.
Debug.step6FormulaDesc: Portfolio variance = weighted sum of all covariances
Debug.diversificationBenefitText: When assets are not perfectly correlated (ρ < 1), portfolio volatility is lower than the weighted average of individual volatilities. This is the power of diversification.
